Repo: /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay (branch harden/pass-1, fixes applied vs main). Venv: /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/.venv/bin/python.
The file /tmp/pass1_confirm.json is a JSON array of 22 pass-1 findings (each: issue, title, file, sev, problem, suggested_fix).
Constraints (NOT bugs): local-first/data-sovereign, no metered API, engine imports no adapter, lazy optional backends.

You are independent CONFIRMER #2 of 2 for finding INDEX 1 (0-based) in /tmp/pass1_confirm.json.
Read that item (its issue number, problem, suggested_fix, file). Then VERIFY THE APPLIED FIX on the current branch: read the changed source AND its regression test (git diff vs main, or just read the file + tests), and where useful run a PoC against the venv to confirm the original problem is actually resolved.
Return: appropriate = the fix correctly addresses the root cause in a sound way (not a hack/incomplete patch/over-claim); complete = the fix fully resolves the finding AND has a regression test that would catch a regression. Set either false if not, and say exactly what is missing. Echo the issue number in 'issue'.

- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

Consider also committing a constraints/lock file (e.g. `pip-compile` output or `uv.lock`) for the heavy extras so installs are reproducible and audited, while keeping pyproject ranges permissive for libr"}, {"issue": "109", "title": "A raised run leaves a partial provenance trail with no failure entry — the abort reason is unrecoverable from the trail", "file": "/Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/pipeline.py", "sev": "MEDIUM", "problem": "GOVERNANCE.md §3 and the docstring at lines 102-103 explicitly promise that "a caller may pass its own trail to run_study so a run that *raises* (a blocked gate, a firewall violation) still leaves an auditable partial trail." Now I understand the full picture. The original problem: `run_json` looped `MAX_RETRIES+1` (=3) times, each calling `_run_with_retries` which itself does up to MAX_RETRIES transient + RATE_LIMIT_MAX_RETRIES rate retries → budgets multiply (e.g. 9 transient attempts, 1350s+ of rate backoff).

The fix:
- `run_json` outer loop is now `JSON_REROLLS + 1` (a *separate, independent* budget), not `MAX_RETRIES+1`. So the outer loop is no longer tied to the same retry constant — semantically it's now "re-roll on parse failure" with its own budget.
- However, `_run_with_retries` is STILL called inside (not `_attempt` directly). So transient/rate-limit errors are still handled by the inner layer, and then if those exhaust they raise `ReasoningError`, which the outer loop catches and re-rolls again. This means the budgets technically *can still multiply*: inner does up to MAX_RETRIES transient retries, then raises, then outer re-rolls up to JSON_REROLLS times → total transient attempts = (MAX_RETRIES+1) * (JSON_REROLLS+1) = 3*3 = 9. Same multiplicative count.

BUT the suggested fix explicitly offered option (b): "keep `_run_with_retries` and have run_json only re-roll on JSON parse failures (not on transient errors the inner loop already handled). Add an absolute wall-clock deadline." The key safeguard against the *retry storm / unbounded blocking* is the deadline, which is checked at the top of each re-roll iteration.

Let me verify the deadline actually bounds the storm. The deadline check is `if attempt > 0 and time.monotonic() >= deadline`. This is checked only at the START of each outer iteration, not inside `_run_with_retries`. So a single `_run_with_retries` call can still run for the full inner budget (1350s rate backoff + work) within one outer iteration before the deadline is even checked. The deadline only prevents the *next* re-roll. Let me check whether the re-roll on transient errors is actually avoided.

Looking at line 596-599: `try: return extract_json(_run_with_retries(...)) except ReasoningError as exc: last = exc`. This catches BOTH parse errors (from `extract_json`) AND transient/rate errors that escaped `_run_with_retries`. So it does NOT distinguish parse failures from transient failures — contrary to suggested-fix option (b)'s "only re-roll on JSON parse failures". Let me check what `extract_json` raises.

Now it's clear. With an *advancing* clock (the realistic case), the deadline DOES short-circuit subsequent re-rolls — so the total is bounded to roughly one inner exhaustion (450s) plus whatever the deadline allows. The earlier PoC2 had a non-advancing clock (sleep didn't advance monotonic), so it ran all 3 re-rolls = 1350s.

So the fix's behavior in reality:
- The deadline DOES prevent the multiplicative re-roll storm across outer iterations (good — that's the main complaint about "1350s × outer iterations").
- BUT a single inner `_run_with_retries` exhaustion (450s of rate backoff + work) can still complete fully even when it exceeds the requested deadline, because the deadline is never checked *during* the inner loop. So the "absolute wall-clock deadline" does not truly bound a single call (450s when you asked for 5s).

Now let me assess against the two criteria carefully:

**Multiplicative retry storm (primary HIGH concern):** The finding's core complaint was budgets *multiplying* across the nested loops (9 transient attempts, 1350s+ backoff). The fix:
- Still has 9 transient attempts in the worst case (PoC1 confirmed). The transient multiplication is NOT eliminated. However, transient retries with `BACKOFF_BASE**transient` (1.5^0+1.5^1 ≈ 2.4s per inner exhaustion) are cheap, and the deadline bounds the total. The more serious rate-limit storm is now bounded by the deadline to ~one inner exhaustion (450s) rather than 1350s+.
- The deadline is a real, new, shared wall-clock bound that prevents the unbounded re-roll multiplication. This is exactly suggested-fix option (b) + the deadline.

**But:** the suggested fix also said "have run_json only re-roll on JSON *parse* failures (not on transient errors the inner loop already handled)." The fix does NOT do this — it catches all `ReasoningError` (including transient/rate errors that escaped the inner loop) and re-rolls. So transient errors trigger a re-roll, which re-enters the full inner budget. This is why PoC1 shows 9 attempts instead of ~3. This is a partial implementation of the suggested fix.

Let me check whether the deadline is checked tightly enough. The deadline default is 600s, and a single inner rate exhaustion is 450s, so within budget the inner loop fits — but it's checked between re-rolls. Worst realistic worst-case with default config: attempt 0 inner = up to 450s + 3×330s work = ~1440s before first deadline check... actually work timeout. Let me assess whether the deadline truly caps. The deadline is 600s default but a single inner iteration can take up to 450s(rate) + ... so first iteration alone can blow past 600s and the deadline only catches it at the START of attempt 1. So the absolute cap is "one inner iteration's worst-case" which can exceed RUN_JSON_DEADLINE.

Now my verdict. Let me summarize the determination.

The fix is a reasonable, sound approach (option b + deadline) and meaningfully reduces the storm: the rate-limit backoff no longer multiplies to 1350s — it's bounded by the deadline to ~one inner exhaustion. It is NOT a hack. There IS a regression test (`test_run_json_overall_deadline_bounds_rerolls`) that would catch a regression of the deadline short-circuit.

However:
- **`appropriate`**: The fix addresses the root cause soundly enough (decouples the budgets conceptually via separate constant, adds the deadline that bounds the storm). I'll lean appropriate=true since it's a defensible, non-hacky implementation of the suggested option (b). 
- **`complete`**: The fix does NOT fully resolve the finding. (1) The transient multiplication remains (9 attempts, PoC confirmed) because it re-rolls on transient errors, contrary to the suggested fix's "only re-roll on parse failures." (2) The "absolute wall-clock deadline" does not actually bound a single call's blocking time — it's checked only between re-rolls, never during the inner backoff loop, so a single call can block 450s when the deadline was 5s (PoC confirmed). The regression test only asserts the between-re-roll short-circuit; it does NOT catch the case where a single inner loop exceeds the deadline, nor the transient multiplication. So complete=false.
